section.title: Righteousness Through Faith in Christ
section.sectionRationale: Paul warns sharply against those who would put confidence in the flesh and add law-keeping to the gospel. He lists his own unmatched religious credentials, then counts them all as loss, indeed as rubbish, for the surpassing worth of knowing Christ. This is the letter's clearest statement of justification: a righteousness not our own from the law but the righteousness from God that is through faith in Christ, whom Paul longs to know in the power of His resurrection and the fellowship of His sufferings.
section.thesis: Against all confidence in the flesh, Paul counts his flawless religious pedigree as loss and rubbish compared to the surpassing worth of knowing Christ, so that he may be found in Him with a righteousness that is not his own from the law but the righteousness from God through faith, knowing Christ and the power of His resurrection and the fellowship of His sufferings.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the warning against confidence in the flesh; Paul's impeccable credentials counted as loss for Christ; and the righteousness from God through faith, and the surpassing worth of knowing Christ.

units.label: Warning against confidence in the flesh
units.note: the true circumcision worship by the Spirit

verses.text: Finally, my brothers, rejoice in the Lord. It is no trouble for me to write the same things to you again, and it is a safeguard for you.
Watch out for those dogs, those workers of evil, those {{katatome|mutilators of the flesh}}!
For it is we who are the circumcision, we who worship by the Spirit of God, who glory in Christ Jesus, and who put no confidence in the flesh,

units.label: Credentials counted as loss
units.note: gain turned to loss for Christ

verses.text: though I myself could have such confidence. If anyone else thinks he has grounds for confidence in the flesh, I have more:
circumcised on the eighth day, of the people of Israel, of the tribe of Benjamin; a Hebrew of Hebrews; as to the law, a Pharisee;
as to zeal, persecuting the church; as to righteousness in the law, faultless.
But whatever was gain to me I count as {{zemia|loss}} for the sake of Christ.

units.label: The righteousness from God through faith

verses.text: More than that, I count all things as loss compared to the surpassing excellence of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord, for whom I have lost all things. I consider them {{skybala|rubbish}}, that I may gain Christ
and be found in Him, not having my own righteousness from the law, but that which is through faith in Christ, the {{dikaiosyne|righteousness}} from God on the basis of faith.
I want to {{ginosko|know}} Christ and the power of His resurrection and the fellowship of His sufferings, being conformed to Him in His death,
and so, somehow, to attain to the resurrection from the dead.

katatome.gloss: A cutting down, a mutilation. Paul makes a stinging wordplay: the false teachers boast in peritome, circumcision, but Paul calls their work katatome, mere mutilation, a cutting of the flesh that counts for nothing before God. True circumcision is of the heart, by the Spirit.

zemia.gloss: Loss, forfeit, damage, as of cargo thrown overboard in a storm. Paul takes everything the world would call gain, his pedigree and achievements, and moves it firmly into the loss column for the sake of Christ. What he once counted as profit he now reckons as debt.

skybala.gloss: Refuse, garbage, dung, what is thrown to the dogs. A deliberately crude word: Paul says his best religious achievements are rubbish compared to Christ. The point is not that his heritage was evil but that it is worthless as a ground of standing before God next to knowing Him.

dikaiosyne.gloss: Righteousness, right standing before God. Paul contrasts two kinds: his own righteousness from the law, which he renounces, and the righteousness from God received through faith in Christ. Justification is God's gift, a right standing credited to those who trust Christ, not earned by works of the law.

ginosko.gloss: To know, not merely to know about but to know personally, intimately, by experience. Paul's supreme desire is to know Christ Himself, to experience the power of His resurrection and even the fellowship of His sufferings. Saving faith issues in a growing, personal knowledge of the living Lord.

basic.context: Paul warns the church about teachers who insisted that Gentile believers must be circumcised and keep the law to be right with God. If anyone could boast in such credentials, Paul says, it is him: born into the right family, trained as a Pharisee, faultless in law-keeping. Yet he throws it all away. Compared to knowing Christ, his religious resume is not just worthless but rubbish. What he wants is Christ, and a righteousness that is God's gift through faith, not his own achievement.
basic.hermeneutics: This is Paul's own testimony pressed into service against a false gospel. He uses his story to teach the heart of justification: right standing with God comes as a gift through faith in Christ, never by pedigree or performance. The passage is autobiography in the service of doctrine.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul mocks the false teachers' katatome, mutilation, over against true circumcision of the heart. All his gains he now counts as zemia, loss, even skybala, rubbish, for Christ. He renounces his own righteousness for the dikaiosyne, righteousness, that is from God through faith, longing to ginosko, personally know, Christ and the power of His resurrection.
basic.typology: Circumcision, the sign of the old covenant, finds its true meaning in the heart circumcised by the Spirit, as the prophets foretold. Paul's death to his own righteousness and life in Christ's fulfills the promise of a new covenant people justified by faith, the true Israel who worship by the Spirit.
basic.christ: Christ is the surpassing treasure for whom everything else is loss. Right standing with God is found only in Him, through faith, not the law. Paul longs to know Him, to share the power of His resurrection and the fellowship of His sufferings, being conformed to His death in hope of resurrection. Christ is both the righteousness we receive and the person we long to know.
basic.god: God is the giver of righteousness. The right standing Paul craves does not come from the law but from God, credited to faith. God is the one worth losing all things to gain, and the source of the resurrection power Paul longs to experience. Salvation is God's gift, freely given, not a wage earned.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 3 and 4 teach righteousness by faith apart from works. Galatians 2:16 says no one is justified by works of the law. Jeremiah 31 promises a new covenant written on the heart.
basic.oneStory: The whole Bible moves from a righteousness sought through law and pedigree to the righteousness God gives freely in Christ. Paul, the model Pharisee, embodies the turn: everything the old system prized is loss beside knowing Christ, in whom God's long promise of a justified, Spirit-worshiping people comes true.

context.literary: A sharp shift in tone marks the section: three staccato warnings in verse 2, then a reversal as Paul lists his own credentials only to cross them all out in the loss column. Verses 7 to 11 build to the climactic contrast of two righteousnesses and the longing to know Christ, held together by the repeated language of gain and loss.
context.historical: The dogs and mutilators are widely understood as Judaizers, teachers who pressed circumcision and law-keeping on Gentile Christians as necessary for salvation, the same error Paul confronts in Galatians. Paul answers not by belittling his Jewish heritage but by relativizing it utterly before Christ.
context.canonical: This is one of the New Testament's central statements of justification by faith, standing with Romans 3 to 4 and Galatians 2 to 3. Its contrast between a righteousness of one's own from the law and the righteousness from God through faith is a cornerstone of the gospel the Reformers recovered.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To guard the church against a works-based, flesh-confident false gospel by holding up his own unmatched credentials, renouncing them as loss for Christ, and setting forth the true ground of standing before God: the righteousness that comes from God through faith in Christ, whom he longs to know.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Paul describes his personal reckoning, counting his gains as loss, but the doctrine beneath it is prescriptive for all: right standing with God is by faith in Christ, not by law or pedigree. The longing to know Christ and share His sufferings is held out as the normal shape of Christian desire.

debates.question: Is the righteousness through faith in Christ our faith in Him or Christ's own faithfulness?

views.label: Faith in Christ received by the believer
views.case: The natural reading is that righteousness comes to those who trust Christ; Paul contrasts his own works-righteousness with a righteousness received through faith, credited by God to the believer.

views.label: The faithfulness of Christ
views.case: Some render the phrase the faithfulness of Christ, grounding righteousness in Christ's own faithful obedience; the BSB notes this alternative, and both readings exalt Christ as the sole basis.

debates.bearing: Either way righteousness is God's gift centered on Christ, received not achieved; the passage rules out any confidence in the flesh.

debates.question: What does Paul mean by so, somehow, to attain the resurrection from the dead?

views.label: Humble certainty, not doubt
views.case: The tentative wording expresses Paul's humility about the path of suffering, not uncertainty about the outcome, which the gospel guarantees to all who are in Christ.

views.label: Genuine uncertainty of final salvation
views.case: Read as doubt, it would clash with 1:6 and the whole letter's assurance; the somehow reflects the mystery of the road, not the security of the destination.

debates.bearing: The believer's resurrection is sure in Christ; Paul's language conveys reverent humility about sharing Christ's sufferings, not anxiety about being finally saved.

study.originalLanguages: Paul's warning turns on a bitter pun: the false teachers prize peritome, circumcision, but he brands their work katatome, mutilation, a mere cutting of flesh. He then audits his life in commercial terms. Everything once in the profit column he now reckons as zemia, loss, and more pointedly skybala, refuse or dung, thrown to the dogs, when set beside Christ. The heart of the passage is dikaiosyne, righteousness: not his own from the law but the righteousness from God received through faith. And his consuming aim is to ginosko Christ, to know Him personally and experientially, including the power of His resurrection and the fellowship of His sufferings.

typology.fulfillment: The old covenant sign is fulfilled in the heart circumcised by the Spirit, marking the true people of God who worship by the Spirit and glory in Christ.
Fulfills Abraham's faith counted as righteousness and the new covenant promise, realized in the right standing God gives through faith in Christ.
The believer conformed to Christ's death and destined for His resurrection fulfills the pattern of dying and rising secured in Christ Himself.

study.christ: Christ is the surpassing treasure who makes everything else look like loss. In Him alone is right standing with God, received through faith, never earned by law or heritage. Paul's deepest desire is not merely to be forgiven but to know Christ personally, to experience the power that raised Him, to share even in His sufferings, being shaped into the likeness of His death in the sure hope of resurrection. Christ is both the righteousness credited to us and the living Lord we are made to know and love.
study.god: God is the source of the only righteousness that avails, a right standing that comes from Him and is received by faith, not manufactured by human effort. He is the God worth losing everything to gain, the one whose resurrection power Paul longs to know. The passage magnifies a God who justifies the ungodly freely in Christ and calls His people into intimate knowledge of Himself.

crossReferences.note: The righteousness of God through faith in Jesus Christ apart from the law, the doctrine Paul states here in testimony.
No one is justified by works of the law but through faith in Christ, the same contrast.
The new covenant written on the heart, fulfilled in a people who worship by the Spirit.
United with Christ in His death and resurrection, matching being conformed to His death and attaining resurrection.

study.oneStory: The Bible's story runs from a righteousness pursued through law, pedigree, and the flesh to the righteousness God gives freely in Christ. Paul, the flawless Pharisee, dramatizes the turn: all the old gains are rubbish beside knowing Christ. In him God's ancient promise of a justified, heart-circumcised, Spirit-worshiping people comes true, and the road leads through fellowship with Christ's sufferings to the resurrection of the dead.

quiz.q: Why does Paul list his impressive religious credentials?

options.text: To show that even the best human righteousness is loss compared to knowing Christ.
options.note: Verses 4 to 8 pile up his credentials only to count them all as loss for Christ.

options.text: To prove he had earned his salvation.
options.note: He renounces this very thing; righteousness is God's gift, not earned.

options.text: To boast about his superiority over others.
options.note: He lists them precisely to renounce boasting in the flesh.

options.text: To encourage others to trust their own pedigree.
options.note: He warns against confidence in the flesh, the opposite of trusting pedigree.

quiz.q: What does Paul count his former gains as, compared to Christ?

options.text: Loss, indeed rubbish, for the surpassing worth of knowing Christ.
options.note: Verses 7 to 8 call them loss and rubbish next to knowing Christ.

options.text: Still valuable achievements worth keeping.
options.note: He moves them all into the loss column for Christ.

options.text: Slightly less important than before.
options.note: He calls them rubbish, not merely less important.

options.text: The true basis of his standing before God.
options.note: He renounces his own righteousness for the righteousness from God.

quiz.q: What two kinds of righteousness does Paul contrast in verse 9?

options.text: His own righteousness from the law, and the righteousness from God through faith.
options.note: Verse 9 sets self-made law-righteousness against God's gift received by faith.

options.text: A greater and a lesser righteousness both earned by works.
options.note: One is not earned at all; it is the righteousness from God through faith.

options.text: Jewish righteousness and Gentile righteousness.
options.note: The contrast is law-righteousness versus God's gift, not ethnicity.

options.text: There is no real contrast; they are the same.
options.note: Paul sharply distinguishes his own righteousness from God's gift.

quiz.q: What is Paul's deepest desire in this passage?

options.text: To know Christ and the power of His resurrection and the fellowship of His sufferings.
options.note: Verse 10 states his consuming aim to know Christ personally.

options.text: To be admired for his religious achievements.
options.note: He counts those achievements as rubbish; his desire is Christ.

options.text: To avoid all suffering at any cost.
options.note: He longs even for the fellowship of Christ's sufferings.

options.text: To return to confidence in the flesh.
options.note: He renounces confidence in the flesh entirely.

quiz.q: How does a person receive the righteousness from God?

options.text: As a gift, through faith in Christ, not by works of the law.
options.note: Verse 9 grounds right standing in faith, not law-keeping.

options.text: By keeping the law faultlessly.
options.note: Paul was faultless in the law yet counts it loss; righteousness is by faith.

options.text: By being born into the right family.
options.note: His pedigree is exactly what he renounces as a basis.

options.text: By a mix of faith and personal merit.
options.note: It is the righteousness from God on the basis of faith, not merit.

questions: Paul counted his best achievements as loss for Christ. What are you tempted to lean on for your standing with God instead of Christ alone?
Righteousness comes as a gift through faith, not by performance. How would resting in a righteousness you did not earn change the way you relate to God?
Paul called his religious resume rubbish next to knowing Christ. Is there anything good in your life you are treasuring above Christ Himself?
Paul longed to know Christ personally, not just to know about Him. What would growing in personal knowledge of Christ look like for you this season?
Paul was willing to share in Christ's sufferings to know Him more. How does the hope of resurrection make that kind of fellowship with Christ worth it?

translationNotes.note: Mutilators of the flesh renders katatome, a deliberate pun on peritome, circumcision; Paul brands mere flesh-cutting as worthless mutilation before God.
The BSB ends this verse with an em dash before verse 4; here it is rendered with a comma to keep the flow, with no change to the wording.
Through faith in Christ renders the phrase the BSB footnotes as possibly through the faithfulness of Christ; both readings ground righteousness wholly in Christ, received not achieved.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Philippians 3:1-11 this session. The em dash at the end of verse 3 is rendered as a comma to comply with the no-dash style rule; wording is unchanged.
